title: Header Files
description: What a header file is, declarations vs definitions, why header/source files are separated, and common Standard Library headers.
objectives: Understand what a header file is and why we use one
Understand the difference between a declaration and a definition
Understand why projects separate .h/.hpp header files from .cpp source files
Recognize common Standard Library headers used in DSA
Understand where header inclusion fits in the compilation process

sections.text: 1. What is a Header File?
A header file provides information about useful C++ features so that our program knows those features exist and how they can be used.
A header file is a file that contains declarations and definitions that can be made available to other C++ source files.

<iostream> refers to a header from the C++ Standard Library. It provides declarations related to input and output streams — because of iostream, we can use standard stream features such as cout and cin (we will learn both separately).
2. Why Do We Use Header Files?

sections.title: Imagine you create a calculator needing

sections.text: Now imagine writing the same code for these operations again and again in every program — this creates repeated code and poor organization. Instead, related programming features can be organized separately.
Header files help organize declarations and make reusable program interfaces available to source files.
3. Real-Life Example

sections.title: The restaurant menu analogy
sections.text: A menu says: Burger, Pizza, Pasta, Coffee — it tells you these items are available. You don't need to enter the kitchen and inspect the complete cooking process before ordering. A header file is similar: it can tell your C++ source file that these functions, classes, objects, and types exist. The detailed implementation may exist elsewhere.
Header = Information about available programming features
4. Header File Extension
User-created C++ header files commonly use extensions such as .h or .hpp. You may see both in C++ projects.
This separation becomes very useful in larger programs.
5. What is a Declaration?
You have not learned functions, parameters, or data types properly yet — you are not expected to write this. Only understand the concept.
This declaration conceptually tells the compiler: there is a function named add. It receives two integer values. It returns an integer value. The actual function code may exist somewhere else.
A declaration tells the compiler that a name or feature exists and provides information about it.
6. What is a Definition?

sections.title: The job advertisement analogy
sections.text: A job advertisement says: Job: Software Developer, Work: Build applications, Experience: 2 years — this is a Declaration (information). The employee actually performing the work is the Definition (actual implementation).
A definition provides the actual implementation or creates the entity being declared, depending on the C++ feature.
Declaration → WHAT EXISTS? Definition → ACTUAL IMPLEMENTATION

Imagine we create calculator.h containing a declaration, and calculator.cpp containing the definition:

sections.text: 8. Why Separate Header and Source Files?
Suppose everything is inside main.cpp — maybe 5,000 lines of code. That becomes difficult to manage. Instead, we can organize the project into logical parts.
9. Standard Library Headers

C++ provides many standard headers. You will see several of them during DSA — these are examples only, you are not expected to use them yet. We will learn every required STL header and feature before using it in a solution.

rows: unordered_map — extremely important during Hashing and LeetCode

sections.text: 10. Why Do Different Features Need Different Headers?

sections.title: The toolbox analogy
sections.text: Imagine an Electrical Toolbox, a Plumbing Toolbox, and a Carpentry Toolbox. If you need a screwdriver for electrical work, you open the appropriate toolbox. Standard headers organize related features the same way: iostream → Input/Output Streams, vector → Vector Container, algorithm → Standard Algorithms, string → String Features.
Header = Toolbox of related programming declarations
11. Understanding <iostream>
The name comes from I/O Stream, where I = Input, O = Output. A stream can be understood as a flow of data.
The iostream header provides the relevant standard stream declarations.
12. Header File vs Source File

rows: Can be included by source files
Compiled as part of the program

sections.text: HEADER → WHAT IS AVAILABLE? SOURCE → HOW IS IT IMPLEMENTED?

This is a simplified beginner-friendly model. In real C++, headers can also contain definitions — for example, templates are commonly defined in header files. We will understand that when we reach Templates.
13. Header Files and the Compilation Process
Header inclusion is handled during Preprocessing. The preprocessor handles #include <iostream> before the main compilation stage.
This is why headers are connected to the preprocessing stage we learned earlier.
14. Are Header Files Executable?
No. A header file such as calculator.h is not normally a standalone executable program — you do not run calculator.h like calculator.exe.
HEADER → PROVIDES INFORMATION. SOURCE → CONTAINS CODE. EXECUTABLE → RUNS.
15. Simple Project Dry Run
Imagine our project contains main.cpp, calculator.cpp, and calculator.h.

Remember our previous lesson: Compiler translates. Linker connects. Now you can see why linking matters — the implementation may exist in another source file, and the linker helps connect the compiled pieces.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Thinking a header file is an executable — iostream is a Standard Library header, not a program that runs.
Thinking header files only contain code implementation — headers commonly contain declarations, but C++ headers can also contain definitions (don't memorize that they never can).
Confusing .cpp and .h — .cpp is a C++ source file, .h / .hpp is a header file.
Thinking iostream means only output — it means Input/Output Stream; later we use cin for input and cout for output.
Thinking the header always contains the complete implementation — sometimes it exists in a separate .cpp file, though templates are often defined directly in headers.
Using a library feature without its required declaration being available — for example, using vector without including the appropriate standard header means the compiler may not know the declaration.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a header file in C++?
questions.answer: A header file is a file that commonly contains declarations and interface information that can be made available to other C++ source files.

questions.question: Why are header files used?
questions.answer: Header files help organize declarations, provide reusable interfaces, and allow multiple source files to use shared programming features.

questions.question: What is the difference between a declaration and a definition?
questions.answer: A declaration tells the compiler that an entity exists and describes it, while a definition provides the actual implementation or creates the entity.

questions.question: What is iostream?
questions.answer: iostream is a C++ Standard Library header that provides declarations for standard input and output stream functionality.

questions.question: What are common C++ header file extensions?
questions.answer: Common C++ header file extensions are .h and .hpp.

questions.question: What is the difference between a header file and a source file?
questions.answer: A header file commonly provides declarations and interfaces, while a source file commonly contains program implementations and is compiled as part of the program.

questions.question: Can a header file contain definitions?
questions.answer: Yes. Header files can contain definitions — for example, template definitions are commonly placed in header files.

questions.question: During which stage is #include handled?
questions.answer: The #include preprocessor directive is handled during the preprocessing stage.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A header file in C++ commonly contains declarations and interface information that can be shared across source files. Header files help organize code and make reusable features available to different parts of a program. Standard Library headers such as iostream provide declarations for standard input and output streams. Header inclusion is handled by the preprocessor before compilation.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Header Files
sections.text: HEADER = MENU — a menu tells you what is available. Declaration → WHAT EXISTS? Definition → HOW IT WORKS? Header = Interface / Information. Source = Implementation / Code.
